title: Death rate from road injuries
subtitle: The annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people. Deaths include those from drivers and passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians.
caption:
Death rate from road injuries, 2021

The annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people. Deaths include those from drivers and passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians.

The choropleth map displays the 2021 annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people across the globe, covering fatalities among drivers, passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists, and pedestrians. The color scale ranges from light beige for rates near zero to dark red for rates reaching 70 deaths per 100,000. The highest values appear concentrated in specific regions of Africa and Asia. Algeria stands out in North Africa with a rate falling in the 40-50 band. In West Africa, countries such as Mali and Guinea-Bissau show rates in the 30-40 range. Moving to Southern Africa, South Africa and Namibia fall within the 20-30 band. In Asia, North Korea is shaded in the darkest red, indicating a rate in the highest bracket of 60-70 deaths per 100,000. Thailand and Vietnam in Southeast Asia display rates in the 30-40 range. Conversely, large portions of the world show significantly lower mortality rates. Most of Western and Northern Europe, including the United Kingdom, France, Germany, and the Nordic countries, along with Canada, Australia, and New Zealand, fall in the lowest band of 0-10 deaths per 100,000. The United States and Brazil sit in the 10-20 band. Greenland is marked with hatching to indicate no data is available. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of higher road injury death rates in parts of Africa and Asia, while North America, Europe, and Oceania generally display lower rates.
